name: CAP Theorem Interview Prep
description: 20 technical questions on the CAP theorem, distributed consensus, and trade-offs in distributed data systems.

cards.front: 1. What does each letter in CAP stand for?
cards.back: Consistency (every read receives the most recent write), Availability (every request receives a non-error response), and Partition Tolerance (the system continues to operate despite network failures).

cards.front: 2. Why is Partition Tolerance considered non-negotiable in distributed systems?
cards.back: Because network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems; if you do not handle them (P), your system will crash or lose data during any network glitch.

cards.front: 3. Does CAP mean you must choose between C and A in all scenarios?
cards.back: No. You only face the trade-off during a network partition. When the network is healthy, a well-designed system can provide both C and A.

cards.front: 4. What is the difference between "Consistency" in CAP and "ACID Consistency"?
cards.back: CAP consistency refers to "Linearizability" (all nodes see the same data at the same time); ACID consistency refers to the database transitioning from one valid state to another, maintaining invariants.

cards.front: 5. What is an AP system and when is it preferred?
cards.back: An AP system favors Availability and Partition Tolerance. It is preferred for systems where high uptime and low latency are critical (e.g., shopping carts, social media feeds).

cards.front: 6. What is a CP system and when is it preferred?
cards.back: A CP system favors Consistency and Partition Tolerance. It is preferred for financial systems or inventory management where stale data could lead to business errors.

cards.front: 7. What does "Eventual Consistency" imply for an AP system?
cards.back: It implies that while the system remains available during a partition, it guarantees that replicas will converge to the same value *eventually* once the partition is resolved.

cards.front: 8. Can a distributed system be CA?
cards.back: Technically, yes, but only if the network never partitions. In real-world distributed systems, a CA system is a "single-node" database, as it cannot survive network failures.

cards.front: 9. How do you resolve conflicts in AP systems after a partition is healed?
cards.back: Use techniques like Last-Write-Wins (LWW), Vector Clocks, or Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs).

cards.front: 10. What is a "Read Repair"?
cards.back: A strategy where the system checks data across multiple replicas during a read; if a mismatch is found (due to a previous partition), it corrects it on the fly.

cards.front: 11. What is the role of a "Quorum" in balancing C and A?
cards.back: Quorums (e.g., $W+R > N$) allow you to tune the consistency level. By increasing the read/write quorum, you favor Consistency; by decreasing them, you favor Availability.

cards.front: 12. Explain the "PACELC" theorem in relation to CAP.
cards.back: PACELC states that even when there is no partition (E), there is still a trade-off between Latency (L) and Consistency (C).

cards.front: 13. What is "Linearizability"?
cards.back: A strong consistency model where, once a write is acknowledged, any subsequent read must reflect that write or a later one.

cards.front: 14. What are the dangers of "Stale Reads" in an AP system?
cards.back: Inaccurate decision-making, such as a user purchasing an item that is actually out of stock, or displaying outdated account balances.

cards.front: 15. How does the "Paxos" or "Raft" algorithm relate to CAP?
cards.back: They are consensus protocols used to build CP systems. They ensure that a majority of nodes agree on the state, even if some nodes fail or the network is partitioned.

cards.front: 16. What is the impact of high-speed networks on CAP?
cards.back: Faster networks reduce the duration and frequency of partitions, allowing developers to implement stronger consistency models with less impact on perceived availability.

cards.front: 17. How do you decide between a CP and AP database for a new microservice?
cards.back: Evaluate the "Cost of Inconsistency" vs. "Cost of Downtime." If the business cost of a wrong read is high, pick CP; if the cost of not being able to read/write is high, pick AP.

cards.front: 18. What is a "Split-Brain" scenario?
cards.back: A result of a partition where two parts of a system both believe they are the "master" and accept conflicting writes, leading to significant data integrity issues.

cards.front: 19. How does "Fencing" help in CP systems?
cards.back: Fencing tokens prevent a "zombie" node (which was partitioned away) from performing writes on a resource after a new master has been elected.

cards.front: 20. Why is the CAP Theorem often misunderstood?
cards.back: It is often misunderstood as a "pick 2 of 3" menu, when it is actually a constraint on how systems behave specifically during network failure.

author: Gemini
